sections.paragraph:
[so also in pause], with suff. מַלְכִּי, pl. מְלָכִים, once מְלָכִין Pro 31:3, and, by insertion of a mater lectionis א, מְלָאכִים 2Sa 11:1 [כתיב], m.

(1) a king. (Arab. مَلِكُ, مَالِكُ, rarely مَلْكُ Syr. ܡܰܠܟܳܐ.) Followed by a genit. of people or land, as מֶלֶךְ סְדֹם Gen 14:2 מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, מֶלֶךְ יִשְׂרָאֵל; but Jehovah’s king, Psa 2:6 18:51 , is the king of Israel [Messiah] appointed by Jehovah.-The name of king is often applied-(a) to Jehovah, as being the king of Israel, and of individual Israelites, Psa 5:3 10:16 44:5 48:3 (in which passage he is called the great king); 68:25 74:12 84:4 Deu 33:5 or of the whole earth, Psa 47:3, Psa 47:8, compare מֶלֶךְ יַעֲקֹב Isa 41:21 מֶלֶךְ יִשְׂרָאֵל 44:6 , of Jehovah.-(b) to idols, when those who worship them are the speakers, Isa 8:21 Amo 5:26 Zep 1:5, compare βασιλεύς, II. γ́ 351; π́ 233. מֶלֶךְ מְלָכִים king of kings is a title of the king of Babylonia, Eze 26:7 (see Ch.); הַמֶּלֶךְ הַגָּדֹול the great king, a title of the king of Assyria, Isa 36:4 (compare βασιλεὺς ὁ μέγας, μέγας βασιλεύς, often in Aristophanes and Plato, of the king of Persia, and Syr. ܡܠܟܐ ܪܒܐ, in Barhebr. of the Roman emperor). Farther, kings are sometimes introduced as leaders of armies, Job 15:24 18:14 30:15 .

(2) [Melech], pr.n. m. 1Ch 8:35 9:41 . Also with the art. הַמֶּלֶךְ Jer 36:26 38:6 .

מלך

emphat. מַלְכָּא, מַלְכָּה; pl. מַלְכִּין, and (in the Hebrew mode) מַלְכִּים Ezr 4:13, Ch. king. מֶלֶךְ מַלְכַּיָּא king of kings, of the king of Babylonia, Dan 2:37 (compare Eze 26:7); of Persia, Ezr 7:12, the latter of which is now called شا هنشاه Shahinshah, i.e. king of kings. The same title of honour was given to the kings of Egypt, the Moguls, the Parthians (see my Comment. on Isa 10:8), the Ethiopians (see Axum. Inscr. in Buttmann’s Museum für Alterthumswiss. vol. i.).- Dan 7:17, in prophetic language מַלְכִּין kings, is put for kingdoms.
